Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: Consumers can start reading from any point within the retention window using startingPosition: 'TRIM_HORIZON' (start of retained data) or 'LATEST' (new records only).
issues.reason: The phrase 'from any point within the retention window' implies arbitrary positioning (e.g. by timestamp), but Stacktape only exposes two starting positions: TRIM_HORIZON and LATEST. While AWS Kinesis natively supports AT_TIMESTAMP iterators, the Stacktape trigger configuration only allows two values. The phrasing is slightly misleading — 'any point' reads as more granular than TRIM_HORIZON/LATEST.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to: 'Consumers can start reading from the beginning of retained data (TRIM_HORIZON) or from new records only (LATEST).' This avoids implying arbitrary seek capability.

issues.statement: The referenceable parameters section uses a manual markdown table instead of the <ReferenceableParams> component.
issues.reason: The page rhythm instructions specify using <ReferenceableParams resource="..." /> for resource pages that expose referenceable parameters. The draft manually lists the two parameters in a markdown table instead. While factually correct, this deviates from the prescribed component pattern and may miss future parameter additions.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the manual table with <ReferenceableParams resource="kinesis-stream" /> and keep a brief introductory sentence explaining how to use $ResourceParam() with the stream.

issues.statement: “Then, on each Lambda function trigger, set `autoCreateConsumer: true` to create a dedicated stream consumer for that function.”
issues.reason: The source says `autoCreateConsumer` automatically creates a dedicated stream consumer for the integration, not specifically one per Lambda function. The draft’s “for that function” wording is plausible, but more specific than the provided type documentation.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: “Set `autoCreateConsumer: true` on a Kinesis trigger to create a dedicated stream consumer for that integration.”

evidence.quote: Automatically creates a dedicated stream consumer for this integration.

issues.statement: “AWS polls the stream and invokes the function with batches of records.”
issues.reason: The source documents two Kinesis consumption modes: direct polling and stream consumer mode. Saying AWS polls the stream as the general behavior ignores the `autoCreateConsumer` path described in the same source.
issues.suggestedFix: Say: “A Kinesis trigger invokes the Lambda function with batches of records. Direct mode polls each shard; `autoCreateConsumer` uses a dedicated stream consumer.”

evidence.quote:
Records are processed in batches. Two consumption modes:
 * - **Direct**: Polls each shard ~1/sec, throughput shared with other consumers.
 * - **Stream Consumer** (`autoCreateConsumer`): Dedicated connection per shard — higher throughput, lower latency.

issues.statement: “Set `onFailure` to route records that exhaust all retries to an SQS queue or SNS topic for manual inspection.”
issues.reason: The source says `onFailure` sends failed batches, not individual records, after all retry attempts. The distinction matters because Kinesis Lambda event source mappings retry and fail at batch granularity.
issues.suggestedFix: Change “records” to “batches”: “Set `onFailure` to route batches that exhaust all retries to an SQS queue or SNS topic.”

evidence.quote: A destination (SQS queue or SNS topic) for batches that fail after all retry attempts.

issues.statement: “Stacktape does not have a built-in Kinesis Data Firehose resource type.”

issues.suggestedFix: Use source-scoped wording: “The Stacktape resource types shown here include `KinesisStream`, but not a Firehose delivery stream resource.”

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Covers all five source-defined KinesisStreamProps properties (capacityMode, shardCount, retentionPeriodHours, encryption, enableEnhancedFanOut) with grounded defaults stated explicitly throughout.

Capacity mode comparison table gives concrete throughput numbers (1 MB/s write, 2 MB/s read per shard) directly from the source JSDoc, making the decision between ON_DEMAND and PROVISIONED actionable.

The idempotency warning in the Lambda trigger section is critical production guidance — entire batches are retried including already-processed records — and is placed exactly where a developer will be configuring error handling.
Lambda handler example with base64 decoding is immediately copy-pasteable and addresses a common real-world gotcha new consumers encounter.

All five trigger settings (batchSize default 10 / max 10,000; startingPosition default TRIM_HORIZON; maxBatchWindowSeconds max 300 s; bisectBatchOnFunctionError; parallelizationFactor) are sourced accurately from KinesisIntegrationProps.

connectTo section correctly avoids claiming specific STP_* env-var injection for Kinesis — it is not listed in the ResourceAccessProps JSDoc table — and documents $ResourceParam() for passing arn/name instead.
All code examples use TypeScript class-based config inside CodeBlock intellisense with no YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, and no fenced typescript blocks containing defineConfig.

FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (replay, enhanced fan-out, connectTo from container), AWS service use-case (shard mechanics, Firehose, pricing model), and comparison (Kinesis vs SQS, on-demand vs provisioned).
Exactly one ApiReference component with definitionName="KinesisStreamProps" matching the source type exactly.
problems: Missing mention of the 1 MB per-record size limit — a hard production constraint (AWS service behavior) that directly affects record partitioning design and batching strategy. Teams designing producers will hit this ceiling and find no guidance here.

The connectTo compute resource list ('Lambda function, web service, worker service, multi-container workload, or batch job') omits private-service, which is covered by StpContainerWorkload in StpResourceScopableByConnectToAffectingRole. Saying 'any compute resource' then listing an incomplete set is misleading.

The relationship between stream-level enableEnhancedFanOut and trigger-level autoCreateConsumer is underspecified. The source KinesisStreamProps JSDoc says 'Enhanced fan-out consumers are auto-created when a Lambda uses autoCreateConsumer: true' — suggesting the stream-level flag may not be a prerequisite. The page presents both as jointly required without explaining what each independently controls, which could cause confusion when only one is set.

The consumerArn trigger property (an alternative to autoCreateConsumer for referencing a pre-existing enhanced fan-out consumer) is never mentioned. Engineers managing existing consumers outside Stacktape need to know this escape hatch exists.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence note about the 1 MB per-record size limit (an AWS Kinesis constraint) in the Basic example or Capacity modes section — e.g. 'Each record can be up to 1 MB; larger payloads must be split across multiple PutRecord calls or stored externally with a pointer in the record.'

Clarify the independent roles of enableEnhancedFanOut (stream-level capability flag) vs autoCreateConsumer (trigger-level consumer provisioning). The source JSDoc hints these may work independently — state whether enableEnhancedFanOut: true is a prerequisite for autoCreateConsumer: true or not, based on AWS behavior.

Add private-service to the connectTo workload list (it is a StpContainerWorkload subtype and therefore in StpResourceScopableByConnectToAffectingRole), or replace the enumeration with a general statement such as 'any Stacktape compute workload' to avoid enumerating an incomplete set.

Mention consumerArn in the Enhanced fan-out section as the escape hatch for reusing an externally managed enhanced fan-out consumer, since the source explicitly documents it alongside autoCreateConsumer and notes the two are mutually exclusive.

The FAQ answer for monitoring mentions 'iterator age' correctly, but could add that a consistently rising iterator age means consumers are falling behind — and that in on-demand mode AWS will attempt to scale shards automatically while in provisioned mode you must add shards manually. This distinction matters operationally.

Consider noting that Kinesis is not a transactional system — there is no exactly-once guarantee in the base API, only at-least-once — and that deduplication is the application's responsibility. This is the most common production surprise for teams coming from SQS.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph (52 words) is fully self-contained and answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — citable verbatim as an AI snippet.
Capacity mode comparison table gives readers a decisive at-a-glance comparison of ON_DEMAND vs PROVISIONED across scaling, throughput, cost model, and best-fit scenarios.
Every opt-in feature (enhanced fan-out, encryption) has explicit when-to-enable, when-to-skip, and cost/tradeoff guidance — proper decision support, not just configuration.
The idempotency Warning in the Lambda trigger section is high-value practical guidance that readers will not find in the API reference.
Producer and consumer handler code examples are realistic and copy-paste runnable — base64 decoding, JSON parsing, and AWS SDK PutRecord all present.

10-question FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (Firehose support, container workload), AWS service (shards, cost, replay), and comparison (Kinesis vs SQS, on-demand vs provisioned).

All KinesisStreamProps properties (capacityMode, shardCount, retentionPeriodHours, encryption + kmsKeyArn, enableEnhancedFanOut) and all major KinesisIntegration trigger properties are documented with working examples.
No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape configs, no StacktapeConfig import pattern, all config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense /> correctly, single <ApiReference />.
connectTo section correctly avoids claiming specific STP_ env var injection for KinesisStream (not listed in the connectTo JSDoc), directs readers to $ResourceParam() instead.
When NOT to use section is a peer H2 (not nested under When to use), covers all major alternatives (SQS, SNS, EventBridge, direct invocation) with concrete reasoning.
problems: H2 'Triggering a Lambda function' is verb-form and 4 words. The style guide prefers short noun-form H2s (e.g. 'Lambda trigger' or 'Lambda integration'). Minor but inconsistent with the rest of the page.

The Enhanced fan-out section (H2) contains a complete Lambda trigger example with `autoCreateConsumer: true`, `batchSize`, and `startingPosition` — substantially overlapping with the subsequent 'Triggering a Lambda function' section. This creates redundancy that hurts scannability and slightly contradicts single-responsibility section design.

On-demand capacity mode has no dedicated code example. The basic example is the implicit on-demand example, but the Capacity modes section only has an explicit example for provisioned. Minor per-variant depth asymmetry.

The `batchSize: 100` value appears in the enhanced fan-out example without nearby prose explaining it controls the default (10) vs the configured value, violating the property-value-anchor rule slightly. The Lambda trigger section does explain batchSize later, but readers scanning the fan-out section encounter the value without context.
optionalImprovements: Rename H2 'Triggering a Lambda function' to 'Lambda trigger' (or 'Lambda integration') to match the noun-form heading convention used elsewhere on the page.

Consolidate the enhanced fan-out code example to show only `enableEnhancedFanOut: true` on the stream and `autoCreateConsumer: true` on the trigger inline-reference, then explicitly link to the Lambda trigger section for full trigger configuration details — eliminating the duplicate example.

Add one sentence before the provisioned-mode code example that anchors the basic example to on-demand: 'The basic example above already shows on-demand mode — no additional configuration needed.' This closes the per-variant depth gap without adding a redundant code block.

Add a one-sentence prose anchor near `batchSize: 100` in the enhanced fan-out example (e.g. 'batchSize controls how many records are delivered per invocation; the default is 10') so the value is not magic for readers who encounter that example before reading the Lambda trigger section.

verifierResults.summary: The Kinesis Stream draft is well-grounded in the source files. All property names, defaults, enum values, and behaviors match the TypeScript type definitions. Code examples use correct class names and property structures. Links are all valid routes in the navigation index. The connectTo section correctly avoids claiming auto-injected env vars for Kinesis (which is not in the connectTo JSDoc enumeration) and instead uses $ResourceParam(). The only structural issue is a missing ReferenceableParams component.

issues.statement: The page documents referenceable parameters in prose ('A Kinesis stream exposes two referenceable parameters: arn and name') but does not include the <ReferenceableParams resource="kinesis-stream" /> component.
issues.reason: The page rhythm instructions explicitly say: 'Referenceable parameters — <ReferenceableParams resource="..." /> if the resource exposes any.' The source confirms KinesisStreamReferencableParam = 'arn' | 'name', so the component should be included on the page.
issues.suggestedFix: Add <ReferenceableParams resource="kinesis-stream" /> as a dedicated section, either after the 'Connecting to other resources' section or before the API Reference.

positiveFindings: All property names (capacityMode, shardCount, retentionPeriodHours, encryption, enableEnhancedFanOut) match the KinesisStreamProps type definition exactly.
All default values are correctly stated: capacityMode ON_DEMAND, shardCount 1, retentionPeriodHours 24, enableEnhancedFanOut false, encryption.enabled false.
The capacity mode enum values ('ON_DEMAND' | 'PROVISIONED') match the source type.
The retention range (24–8,760 hours) matches the source JSDoc.

Kinesis trigger properties (batchSize default 10/max 10000, startingPosition default TRIM_HORIZON, maxBatchWindowSeconds max 300, autoCreateConsumer, bisectBatchOnFunctionError, maximumRetryAttempts, parallelizationFactor, onFailure) all match KinesisIntegrationProps.

The connectTo section correctly avoids claiming auto-injected environment variables for Kinesis streams (which is not in the connectTo JSDoc enumeration) and instead uses $ResourceParam() — this is exactly right per the source-grounding rules.
The claim that Kinesis streams are valid connectTo targets is supported by StpResourceScopableByConnectToAffectingRole including StpKinesisStream.
KinesisStreamReferencableParam = 'arn' | 'name' is correctly documented in prose.
ApiReference definitionName='KinesisStreamProps' matches the source type name.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index.
Encryption section correctly describes the optional kmsKeyArn and the default AWS-managed alias/aws/kinesis key, matching the source JSDoc.
When to use / When NOT to use are correctly structured as peer H2 sections.
FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison categories.
Code examples use valid Stacktape class names (KinesisStream, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging) from the approved class name list.
All Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with label: 'TypeScript' — no forbidden YAML blocks or fenced TypeScript.
